title: Pleural Tap & Chest Drain
oneLiner: Bedside procedures to diagnose/relieve pleural fluid or air; life-saving in tension pneumothorax, but carry serious iatrogenic risk if not performed with correct anatomy and safety steps.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Tension pneumothorax** — tracheal deviation away, absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, hypotension, distended neck veins. *Do not wait for CXR; immediate needle decompression (2nd intercostal space, mid-clavicular line) then chest drain.*
- **Massive haemothorax** — shock + dull percussion + absent breath sounds after trauma or procedure. *Large-bore drain (at least 28Fr), IV access, cross-match, consider thoracotomy.*
- **Iatrogenic visceral puncture** (liver, spleen, diaphragm) — severe shoulder tip pain, hypotension, abdominal rigidity. *Stop, call senior, urgent USS/CT, consider surgical review.*
- **Re-expansion pulmonary oedema** — sudden cough, frothy sputum, hypoxia after draining >1L fluid rapidly. *Slow drainage (clamp if >1.5L in one sitting), support with oxygen, diurese, +/- CPAP.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Symptomatic pleural effusion:** progressive dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, dull ache, orthopnoea, +/- cough (dry or productive)
- **Pneumothorax:** acute onset sharp chest pain, dyspnoea (severity varies), decreased breath sounds, hyperresonance on percussion, tracheal deviation (if tension)
- **Empyema:** fevers, rigours, productive cough (purulent sputum), pleuritic pain, systemic unwellness, night sweats
- **Haemothorax:** signs of hypovolaemia (tachycardia, hypotension) + dull percussion + minimal breath sounds (usually post-trauma or post-procedure)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Pleural effusion vs consolidation** — effusion gives dull percussion, stony dullness; consolidation gives bronchial breathing, dullness but not stony
- **Pneumothorax vs large bulla** — pneumothorax has no lung markings; bulla has curved inner margin; CXR ± CT for distinction
- **Transudate vs exudate** — Light’s criteria: exudate if protein pleural/serum >0.5, LDH pleural/serum >0.6, or LDH pleural >2/3 upper limit of normal for serum
- **Empyema vs simple parapneumonic effusion** — empyema has frank pus, positive Gram stain/culture, low pH (<7.2), high LDH (>1000)
- **Splitter:** After aspiration, if fluid is clear but pH <7.2 → complicated parapneumonic effusion (needs drainage, not just tap)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Ultrasound (USS) for fluid localisation – mandatory before any pleural tap or drain (BTS guidance). Mark site with patient sitting upright, confirm fluid depth and avoid diaphragm/liver/spleen.
  - Pleural fluid appearance: straw-coloured (transudate), cloudy/purulent (empyema), bloody (trauma, malignancy, pulmonary embolism), milky (chylothorax – check triglycerides).
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, CRP, coagulation screen (INR, APTT) – aim INR <1.5, platelets >50; check before procedure.
  - Pleural fluid: protein, LDH, glucose, pH (blood gas syringe – on ice to lab, anaerobic), Gram stain, culture, ± cytology (if suspected malignancy), ± TB (ADA, culture, GeneXpert)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: erect PA CXR (for pneumothorax – deep sulcus sign, mediastinal shift; for effusion – meniscus sign, blunted costophrenic angle)
  - Second-line: lateral decubitus CXR (for small effusion), CT chest (for loculated effusion, empyema, underlying lung pathology)
- **Special tests:** Light’s criteria (transudate vs exudate), pleural pH (<7.2 suggests complicated parapneumonic/empyema), pleural cholesterol (>60 suggests exudate)
- **Never-miss test:** **Coagulation screen** before any pleural procedure – bleeding risk must be assessed; correct any coagulopathy or thrombocytopenia before proceeding.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Tension pneumothorax:** 14G cannula 2nd ICS MCL, then chest drain (size 16–20Fr for pneumothorax, 24–32Fr for haemothorax/empyema) in 5th ICS MAL, blunt dissection, confirm intrapleural position.
  - **Large symptomatic effusion:** therapeutic tap (aspirate up to 1.5L slowly) or pigtail drain if recurrent.
  - **Empyema:** start IV antibiotics (see Drugs), urgent drainage (chest drain ± intrapleural fibrinolytics).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Chest drain management:** connect to underwater seal (bubbling = air leak; swinging = pleural pressure changes). Document output (colour, volume). Check CXR post-insertion to confirm position. Suction (-5 to -20 cmH2O) only on senior instruction (usually for persistent pneumothorax).
  - **Daily monitoring:** respiratory rate, oxygen saturations, pain score, drain output, signs of infection. Clamp drain for 1–2 hours before removal (if no air leak and output <200mL/24h).
  - **If still bubbling after 5 days:** consider persistent air leak → refer to respiratory/thoracic surgery.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Indwelling pleural catheter (IPC) for recurrent malignant effusion (managed by respiratory team).
  - Surgical pleurodesis (VATS talc poudrage) for recurrent pneumothorax or malignant effusion.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: no specific requirement unless hypovolaemic. Maintain IV access for analgesia.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin (but hold 12h pre/post drain insertion if high bleeding risk – check local policy).
  - Diet/NBM: patient may eat normally unless procedure planned (then NBM 2h for local anaesthetic only). For drain insertion under conscious sedation, NBM 6h.
  - Observations: hourly for 2h post-procedure, then 4–6 hourly. Record drain output daily.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Local anaesthetic:** Lidocaine 1% (max 3 mg/kg) – infiltrate skin, subcutaneous tissue, intercostal muscle, pleura. Use adrenaline-containing lidocaine if no contraindication (e.g. Lidocaine 1% with adrenaline 1:200,000) – reduces systemic absorption and prolongs effect. [Check local policy for max safe dose in your trust]
- **Analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g QDS PO/IV. Consider NSAIDs (e.g. ibuprofen 400mg TDS PO) if no contraindication (e.g. renal impairment, bleeding risk). Opiates (morphine 5–10mg SC/IV PRN) for severe pain.
- **Antibiotics (empyema):** Start empiric co-amoxiclav 1.2g TDS IV (or piperacillin-tazobactam if severely unwell). If penicillin-allergic: ceftriaxone 2g daily IV + metronidazole 500mg TDS IV. [Check local microbiology guidance]
- **Interpleural fibrinolytics (for loculated empyema):** Urokinase 100,000 IU in 100mL NaCl 0.9% instilled via drain, clamped for 1h, then unclamped. [Check local protocol – usually 3 doses over 3 days]
- **Key contraindications/interactions:** Lidocaine – avoid in severe bradycardia, heart block. NSAIDs – avoid if AKI, GI bleed, or on anticoagulants.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Lidocaine – caution in severe hepatic impairment. Co-amoxiclav – reduce dose if eGFR <30. Ceftriaxone – not affected by renal; caution in biliary obstruction.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Lidocaine overdose** – accidental intravascular injection or exceeding max dose can cause seizures, arrhythmias, cardiac arrest. Always aspirate before injecting, and calculate max dose. For a 70kg adult, max 210mg of 1% lidocaine (i.e. 21mL). Never administer without knowing the patient's weight.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Pneumothorax (if tapping effusion – puncture of lung)
  - Haemorrhage (intercostal artery injury – especially in low intercostal space)
  - Re-expansion pulmonary oedema (if >1.5L removed rapidly)
  - Infection (empyema) – strict aseptic technique
  - Visceral puncture (liver, spleen, diaphragm)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chest drain site infection
  - Persistent air leak (>7 days)
  - Fibrothorax (trapped lung)
  - Pain and scarring
sections.Prognosis:
- **Pneumothorax:** recurrences 30% after first episode, 50% after second; definitive treatment (pleurodesis) reduces recurrence to <10%.
- **Empyema:** mortality 10–20% if untreated; with drainage and antibiotics, survival >80% but prolonged hospital stay (weeks).
- **Malignant pleural effusion:** median survival 3–12 months depending on primary tumour.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always use ultrasound before any pleural procedure** – reduces organ puncture risk by >50% (BTS/IR guidance).
- **When inserting a chest drain, use blunt dissection with a clamp** – never use a trocar (high visceral injury risk).
- **Before removing a chest drain, check CXR for residual air/fluid** and ensure no air leak for 24h and output <200mL/day.
- **For diagnostic tap for suspected empyema, send pleural fluid pH in a blood gas syringe** – a pH <7.2 is an immediate indication for drainage.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not perform a pleural procedure without checking coagulation** – INR >1.5 or platelets <50 is a relative contraindication; discuss with senior/haematology.
- **Do not insert a chest drain too low** – the safe triangle (5th ICS MAL) avoids liver/spleen. Never go below the 9th intercostal space posteriorly.
- **Do not connect a chest drain to a suction bottle without checking the suction level** – high negative pressure can cause re-expansion pulmonary oedema or pain. Start low (-10 to -20 cmH2O) and only on senior advice.
- **Do not clamp a chest drain in a bubbling pneumothorax** – may precipitate tension pneumothorax; clamps are only used for short intervals (e.g. changing bottle, transport).
- **Do not forget to label pleural fluid bottles with source (pleural) and send for appropriate tests** – mislabelling leads to misdiagnosis.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening breathlessness, chest pain, fever, crepitus (surgical emphysema), blood-stained or purulent sputum, drain falls out (if still in situ).
- **Follow-up:** CXR in 2–4 weeks to document resolution. If empyema or malignancy, respiratory outpatient follow-up in 2–4 weeks.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- Call senior immediately if:
  - Suspected tension pneumothorax (tracheal deviation, shock, respiratory distress) – perform needle decompression while waiting.
  - Massive haemothorax (drain output >1500mL immediate or >200mL/h for 2–4h)
  - Suspected iatrogenic organ puncture (shoulder tip pain, abdominal pain, hypotension)
  - Re-expansion pulmonary oedema (frothy sputum, hypoxia, tachycardia – call ICU)
- Escalate to ICU if: NEWS2 ≥7, PaO2 <8 kPa on FiO2 >0.6, lactate >4 mmol/L, GCS <9, hypotension not responding to fluids.
- For any chest drain complication (blockage, dislodgement, persistent air leak >5 days) – refer to respiratory or thoracic surgery.

rawMarkdown:
## Pleural Tap & Chest Drain

**One-liner:** Bedside procedures to diagnose/relieve pleural fluid or air; life-saving in tension pneumothorax, but carry serious iatrogenic risk if not performed with correct anatomy and safety steps.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Tension pneumothorax** — tracheal deviation away, absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, hypotension, distended neck veins. *Do not wait for CXR; immediate needle decompression (2nd intercostal space, mid-clavicular line) then chest drain.*
- **Massive haemothorax** — shock + dull percussion + absent breath sounds after trauma or procedure. *Large-bore drain (at least 28Fr), IV access, cross-match, consider thoracotomy.*
- **Iatrogenic visceral puncture** (liver, spleen, diaphragm) — severe shoulder tip pain, hypotension, abdominal rigidity. *Stop, call senior, urgent USS/CT, consider surgical review.*
- **Re-expansion pulmonary oedema** — sudden cough, frothy sputum, hypoxia after draining >1L fluid rapidly. *Slow drainage (clamp if >1.5L in one sitting), support with oxygen, diurese, +/- CPAP.*

### Presentation
- **Symptomatic pleural effusion:** progressive dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, dull ache, orthopnoea, +/- cough (dry or productive)
- **Pneumothorax:** acute onset sharp chest pain, dyspnoea (severity varies), decreased breath sounds, hyperresonance on percussion, tracheal deviation (if tension)
- **Empyema:** fevers, rigours, productive cough (purulent sputum), pleuritic pain, systemic unwellness, night sweats
- **Haemothorax:** signs of hypovolaemia (tachycardia, hypotension) + dull percussion + minimal breath sounds (usually post-trauma or post-procedure)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Pleural effusion vs consolidation** — effusion gives dull percussion, stony dullness; consolidation gives bronchial breathing, dullness but not stony
- **Pneumothorax vs large bulla** — pneumothorax has no lung markings; bulla has curved inner margin; CXR ± CT for distinction
- **Transudate vs exudate** — Light’s criteria: exudate if protein pleural/serum >0.5, LDH pleural/serum >0.6, or LDH pleural >2/3 upper limit of normal for serum
- **Empyema vs simple parapneumonic effusion** — empyema has frank pus, positive Gram stain/culture, low pH (<7.2), high LDH (>1000)
- **Splitter:** After aspiration, if fluid is clear but pH <7.2 → complicated parapneumonic effusion (needs drainage, not just tap)

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Ultrasound (USS) for fluid localisation – mandatory before any pleural tap or drain (BTS guidance). Mark site with patient sitting upright, confirm fluid depth and avoid diaphragm/liver/spleen.
  - Pleural fluid appearance: straw-coloured (transudate), cloudy/purulent (empyema), bloody (trauma, malignancy, pulmonary embolism), milky (chylothorax – check triglycerides).
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, CRP, coagulation screen (INR, APTT) – aim INR <1.5, platelets >50; check before procedure.
  - Pleural fluid: protein, LDH, glucose, pH (blood gas syringe – on ice to lab, anaerobic), Gram stain, culture, ± cytology (if suspected malignancy), ± TB (ADA, culture, GeneXpert)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: erect PA CXR (for pneumothorax – deep sulcus sign, mediastinal shift; for effusion – meniscus sign, blunted costophrenic angle)
  - Second-line: lateral decubitus CXR (for small effusion), CT chest (for loculated effusion, empyema, underlying lung pathology)
- **Special tests:** Light’s criteria (transudate vs exudate), pleural pH (<7.2 suggests complicated parapneumonic/empyema), pleural cholesterol (>60 suggests exudate)
- **Never-miss test:** **Coagulation screen** before any pleural procedure – bleeding risk must be assessed; correct any coagulopathy or thrombocytopenia before proceeding.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Tension pneumothorax:** 14G cannula 2nd ICS MCL, then chest drain (size 16–20Fr for pneumothorax, 24–32Fr for haemothorax/empyema) in 5th ICS MAL, blunt dissection, confirm intrapleural position.
  - **Large symptomatic effusion:** therapeutic tap (aspirate up to 1.5L slowly) or pigtail drain if recurrent.
  - **Empyema:** start IV antibiotics (see Drugs), urgent drainage (chest drain ± intrapleural fibrinolytics).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Chest drain management:** connect to underwater seal (bubbling = air leak; swinging = pleural pressure changes). Document output (colour, volume). Check CXR post-insertion to confirm position. Suction (-5 to -20 cmH2O) only on senior instruction (usually for persistent pneumothorax).
  - **Daily monitoring:** respiratory rate, oxygen saturations, pain score, drain output, signs of infection. Clamp drain for 1–2 hours before removal (if no air leak and output <200mL/24h).
  - **If still bubbling after 5 days:** consider persistent air leak → refer to respiratory/thoracic surgery.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Indwelling pleural catheter (IPC) for recurrent malignant effusion (managed by respiratory team).
  - Surgical pleurodesis (VATS talc poudrage) for recurrent pneumothorax or malignant effusion.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: no specific requirement unless hypovolaemic. Maintain IV access for analgesia.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin (but hold 12h pre/post drain insertion if high bleeding risk – check local policy).
  - Diet/NBM: patient may eat normally unless procedure planned (then NBM 2h for local anaesthetic only). For drain insertion under conscious sedation, NBM 6h.
  - Observations: hourly for 2h post-procedure, then 4–6 hourly. Record drain output daily.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Local anaesthetic:** Lidocaine 1% (max 3 mg/kg) – infiltrate skin, subcutaneous tissue, intercostal muscle, pleura. Use adrenaline-containing lidocaine if no contraindication (e.g. Lidocaine 1% with adrenaline 1:200,000) – reduces systemic absorption and prolongs effect. [Check local policy for max safe dose in your trust]
- **Analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g QDS PO/IV. Consider NSAIDs (e.g. ibuprofen 400mg TDS PO) if no contraindication (e.g. renal impairment, bleeding risk). Opiates (morphine 5–10mg SC/IV PRN) for severe pain.
- **Antibiotics (empyema):** Start empiric co-amoxiclav 1.2g TDS IV (or piperacillin-tazobactam if severely unwell). If penicillin-allergic: ceftriaxone 2g daily IV + metronidazole 500mg TDS IV. [Check local microbiology guidance]
- **Interpleural fibrinolytics (for loculated empyema):** Urokinase 100,000 IU in 100mL NaCl 0.9% instilled via drain, clamped for 1h, then unclamped. [Check local protocol – usually 3 doses over 3 days]
- **Key contraindications/interactions:** Lidocaine – avoid in severe bradycardia, heart block. NSAIDs – avoid if AKI, GI bleed, or on anticoagulants.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Lidocaine – caution in severe hepatic impairment. Co-amoxiclav – reduce dose if eGFR <30. Ceftriaxone – not affected by renal; caution in biliary obstruction.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Lidocaine overdose** – accidental intravascular injection or exceeding max dose can cause seizures, arrhythmias, cardiac arrest. Always aspirate before injecting, and calculate max dose. For a 70kg adult, max 210mg of 1% lidocaine (i.e. 21mL). Never administer without knowing the patient's weight.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Pneumothorax (if tapping effusion – puncture of lung)
  - Haemorrhage (intercostal artery injury – especially in low intercostal space)
  - Re-expansion pulmonary oedema (if >1.5L removed rapidly)
  - Infection (empyema) – strict aseptic technique
  - Visceral puncture (liver, spleen, diaphragm)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chest drain site infection
  - Persistent air leak (>7 days)
  - Fibrothorax (trapped lung)
  - Pain and scarring

### Prognosis
- **Pneumothorax:** recurrences 30% after first episode, 50% after second; definitive treatment (pleurodesis) reduces recurrence to <10%.
- **Empyema:** mortality 10–20% if untreated; with drainage and antibiotics, survival >80% but prolonged hospital stay (weeks).
- **Malignant pleural effusion:** median survival 3–12 months depending on primary tumour.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always use ultrasound before any pleural procedure** – reduces organ puncture risk by >50% (BTS/IR guidance).
- **When inserting a chest drain, use blunt dissection with a clamp** – never use a trocar (high visceral injury risk).
- **Before removing a chest drain, check CXR for residual air/fluid** and ensure no air leak for 24h and output <200mL/day.
- **For diagnostic tap for suspected empyema, send pleural fluid pH in a blood gas syringe** – a pH <7.2 is an immediate indication for drainage.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not perform a pleural procedure without checking coagulation** – INR >1.5 or platelets <50 is a relative contraindication; discuss with senior/haematology.
- **Do not insert a chest drain too low** – the safe triangle (5th ICS MAL) avoids liver/spleen. Never go below the 9th intercostal space posteriorly.
- **Do not connect a chest drain to a suction bottle without checking the suction level** – high negative pressure can cause re-expansion pulmonary oedema or pain. Start low (-10 to -20 cmH2O) and only on senior advice.
- **Do not clamp a chest drain in a bubbling pneumothorax** – may precipitate tension pneumothorax; clamps are only used for short intervals (e.g. changing bottle, transport).
- **Do not forget to label pleural fluid bottles with source (pleural) and send for appropriate tests** – mislabelling leads to misdiagnosis.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening breathlessness, chest pain, fever, crepitus (surgical emphysema), blood-stained or purulent sputum, drain falls out (if still in situ).
- **Follow-up:** CXR in 2–4 weeks to document resolution. If empyema or malignancy, respiratory outpatient follow-up in 2–4 weeks.

### Escalation Criteria
- Call senior immediately if:
  - Suspected tension pneumothorax (tracheal deviation, shock, respiratory distress) – perform needle decompression while waiting.
  - Massive haemothorax (drain output >1500mL immediate or >200mL/h for 2–4h)
  - Suspected iatrogenic organ puncture (shoulder tip pain, abdominal pain, hypotension)
  - Re-expansion pulmonary oedema (frothy sputum, hypoxia, tachycardia – call ICU)
- Escalate to ICU if: NEWS2 ≥7, PaO2 <8 kPa on FiO2 >0.6, lactate >4 mmol/L, GCS <9, hypotension not responding to fluids.
- For any chest drain complication (blockage, dislodgement, persistent air leak >5 days) – refer to respiratory or thoracic surgery.
